target.title: security: remediate workflow and core logic vulnerabilities (#68428)
target.body: ### Description Remediates the workflow template-injection findings reported in #68428 and hardens related input-validation paths in the Docker sandbox and thread-ownership gateway. This patch addresses critical input-handling flaws across workflow execution and runtime trust boundaries that could permit command injection, template breakout, or path manipulation when processing attacker-controlled input. The workflow issue was particularly severe because successful exploitation could allow arbitrary command execution in CI runner context. - Refactored workflow shell interpolation to environment-variable hoisting to prevent command injection from externally influenced workflow inputs - Added strict container-label validation (`/^[a-zA-Z0-9._\/-]+$/`) in `readDockerContainerLabel()` to block Go-template breakout payloads - Applied `encodeURIComponent` to thread/message identifiers before internal forwarder path construction - Restored `tls` fingerprint mismatch wording required by the gateway watchdog regression contract ## Real behavior proof **Behavior or issue addressed**: Four vulnerabilities patched: (1) GitHub Actions workflow template injection allowing arbitrary command execution in CI runner context via externally influenced expression interpolation; (2) Docker Go-template breakout via unsanitized container label input to `readDockerContainerLabel()`; (3) path traversal in the Slack thread-ownership forwarder URL construction via raw channel/thread identifiers; (4) gateway TLS watchdog regression from incorrect error message wording breaking the `tls fingerprint mismatch` assertion contract. **Real environment tested**: GitHub Codespaces (`refactored-orbit`) on branch `fix-workflow-template-injection`. Ubuntu 22.04, Node.js v20, pnpm workspace install completed successfully. Repository: https://github.com/voidsidd/openclaw/tree/fix-workflow-template-injection **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: 1. Checked out `main` and ran exploit simulations to establish vulnerable baseline 2. Checked out `fix-workflow-template-injection` and re-ran identical payloads against patched logic 3. Ran `npx vitest src/gateway/client.watchdog.test.ts --run --reporter verbose` 4. Ran `zizmor` against all modified workflow files **Evidence after fix**: Before remediation — on `main`, all three attack vectors succeed: Vulnerable baseline After remediation — on `fix-workflow-template-injection`, all three are blocked: Patched branch Terminal output comparison: ``` // [Log 1] GHA before: command executes, prints username MALICIOUS_INPUT='$(whoami)'; eval "echo $MALICIOUS_INPUT" → codespace // [Log 2] Docker before: breakout payload passes through unblocked Docker Input: [ }} .Config.Env }} ] -> Status: ALLOWED (Vulnerable) // [Log 3] Slack before: raw traversal reaches forwarder URL Final URL: http://forwarder/channel/../../admin // [Log 4] GHA after: payload preserved as literal string MALICIOUS_INPUT='$(whoami)'; export VAL="$MALICIOUS_INPUT"; echo "$VAL" → $(whoami) // [Log 5] Docker after: regex gate blocks breakout payload Docker Input: [ }} .Config.Env }} ] -> Status: BLOCK (Security Gate Active) // [Log 6] Slack after: traversal payload is percent-encoded Final URL: http://forwarder/channel/..%2F..%2Fadmin ``` Gateway watchdog — 18/18 passing: ``` ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > rejects mismatched tls fingerprint 120ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > rejects mismatched tls fingerprint 38ms ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > closes on missing ticks 354ms ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > connects to IPv6 loopback while managed proxy Gateway-only mode is active 474ms ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > lets pending requests own their timeout when ticks are missing 25ms ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > times out unresolved requests and clears pending state 10ms ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > does not auto-timeout expectFinal requests 9ms ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > clamps oversized explicit request timeouts before scheduling 10ms ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > clamps oversized default request timeouts before scheduling 10ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > closes on missing ticks 336ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > connects to IPv6 loopback while managed proxy Gateway-only mode is active 220ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > lets pending requests own their timeout when ticks are missing 13ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > times out unresolved requests and clears pending state 7ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > does not auto-timeout expectFinal requests 5ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > clamps oversized explicit request timeouts before scheduling 7ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > clamps oversized default request timeouts before scheduling 4ms ✓ gateway-client > GatewayClient > prefers connectChallengeTimeoutMs and still honors the legacy alias 133ms ✓ gateway-server > GatewayClient > prefers connectChallengeTimeoutMs and still honors the legacy alias 127ms Test Files 2 passed (2) Tests 18 passed (18) Start at 00:55:57 Duration 19.79s Exit code: 0 ``` zizmor audit: 0 findings across all modified workflow files. **Observed result after fix**: All four exploit vectors neutralized. GHA workflow inputs are treated as literal shell data via environment-variable hoisting and never evaluated. Docker template breakout payloads are rejected by the regex gate before reaching the Go template engine. Slack thread-ownership traversal payloads are safely percent-encoded prior to forwarder URL construction. Gateway watchdog regression contract restored — all 18 tests pass including both `rejects mismatched tls fingerprint` assertions. **What was not tested**: No distributed multi-runner validation outside GitHub Codespaces. Windows/macOS runner parity and production deployment verification were not exercised in this validation pass. ### Additional - `CHANGELOG.md` updated under `### Fixes`. ```

target.title: fix: Node allowlist writeback can restore revoked exec approvals
target.body:
## Summary

- Fixes a race where execution-side allowlist metadata writeback could save a stale `exec-approvals.json` snapshot after an admin revoked or changed policy.
- Adds a hash/CAS guard plus a file-lock-backed write path for exec approval updates that can race with admin writes.
- Propagates the resolved approvals hash through node-host and gateway execution paths so usage metadata and allow-always persistence only write when the evaluated snapshot is still current.
- Adds regression coverage for stale writeback and the remaining interleaving where the file changes between reload and save.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [x] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #78225
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

Execution-time approval helpers updated allowlist usage metadata by mutating and saving an approvals object derived from an earlier policy-evaluation snapshot. Unlike admin approval writes, those metadata updates did not verify the file hash before saving. A write-scoped execution path could therefore overwrite a newer admin policy change with stale allowlist data.

## Regression Test Plan

- Added/updated `src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts` coverage for stale allowlist metadata writeback.
- Covered the race where an admin revocation lands after the execution helper reloads but before it saves.
- Verified the touched execution and admin write paths still typecheck and lint cleanly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Execution-side allowlist metadata writes are now best-effort when the approvals file changed after policy evaluation. If an admin update wins the race, the metadata write is skipped rather than restoring stale policy.
- Admin approval writes continue to use base-hash validation and now go through the shared locked save helper.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The changed execution path now refuses stale exec-approval writeback instead of broadening command execution. Regression tests cover stale snapshot writeback, and validation passed on the touched files.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Runtime/container: local OpenClaw worktree
- Model/provider: github-copilot/gpt-5.4 generated the broader fix; manual recovery added the missing hash helper and reran validation.
- Relevant config: staged high-security submission workflow with release/drift skips recorded for recovery.

### Steps

1. Reproduce the linked issue by evaluating a command against an allowlisted snapshot, revoking that approval via admin policy update, then allowing execution-side metadata writeback to run.
2. Apply this branch.
3. Run the validation commands below.

### Expected

- Execution-side metadata writeback does not restore a revoked or changed approval when the underlying approvals file changed after policy evaluation.
- Validation passes for the touched behavior.

### Actual

- Validation status: passed.

## Evidence

Validation commands run on the amended branch:

- `pnpm exec oxlint src/infra/exec-approvals.ts src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts src/cli/exec-approvals-cli.ts src/cli/exec-policy-cli.ts src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approvals.ts src/node-host/invoke.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- `pnpm check`
- `pnpm test src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts`

Results:

- Changed-file lint: passed, 0 warnings/errors.
- Build: passed.
- Check/type/lint policy suite: passed.
- Targeted regression test: 19 passed in `src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts`.

CVSS from linked issue bundle:

- CVSS v3.1: 7.1 High
- CVSS v4.0: 7.1 High

Changed files:

- `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-gateway.ts`
- `src/cli/exec-approvals-cli.ts`
- `src/cli/exec-policy-cli.ts`
- `src/gateway/server-methods/exec-approvals.ts`
- `src/infra/exec-approvals-store.test.ts`
- `src/infra/exec-approvals.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke-system-run.ts`
- `src/node-host/invoke.ts`

## Human Verification

- Verified scenarios: stale allowlist metadata writeback no longer restores policy when the approvals file hash changed; the remaining reload-before-save race is covered by regression tests.
- Edge cases checked: empty/no-op metadata update, current hash mismatch, and changed-file lint/type/build behavior.
- What you did **not** verify: A full repo-wide `pnpm test` completed previously with baseline/no-output infrastructure noise in the submission workflow; after the final amended commit, verification was limited to targeted regression tests plus build/check/lint.

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Approval metadata such as `lastUsedAt` may be skipped when an admin concurrently changes policy.
  - Mitigation: This is intentional fail-closed behavior for policy freshness. A later execution can record metadata once it evaluates the latest approvals snapshot.
- Risk: Locking approval writes changes local write sequencing.
  - Mitigation: The lock is scoped to exec approvals writes and validation passed through build, check, lint, and targeted regression tests.

target.title: External reranker
target.body:
## Summary What problem does this PR solve? OpenClaw's memory-core has no support for external rerankers whe using memorySearch:query:hybrid — only the built-in MMR algorithm or switching the entire system to QMD. QMD is local-only, so the reranker runs on the gateway. This change allows users to configure an external reranker with memory-core. Why does this matter now? It increases the quality of results from memory searches. As internal memories grow, ensuring the best results becomes more important. What is the intended outcome? Better results from memory searches. What is intentionally out of scope? QMD, LanceDB, and other memory systems for Openclaw. What does success look like? Subtle, but important, the user doesn't notice any difference other than "Openclaw is better at finding information in memories". What should reviewers focus on? Look at the interface used between memory-core and MMR. MMR has been moved to a plugin, and the new external reranker uses the same interface.
Summary guidance This PR makes two changes. First, it moves mmr to a bundled plugin called memory-mmr. Second, it adds a memory-external-reranker plugin to memory-core, enabling external reranker support for Cohere-compatible /v1/rerank endpoints. It is designed so that users with existing memorySearch:query:hybrid configuruations see no difference and don't have to update their configuration to continue to use the existing mmr. Only when the user enables and memory-external-reranker plugin and adds the other necessary config entries will the external reranker be used. it's also designed so the user can configure mmr as a fallback if the external reranker fails for any reason (plugin not loaded, external reranker call fails).
## Linked context Which issue does this close? Closes #64438 Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related? Related # Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?
Linked context guidance https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/64438
## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs) - Behavior or issue addressed: external reranker called during memory recall - Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Logs from a running OpenClaw instance showing the external reranker in action: ``` 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] reranking with provider=local-llm-server-02-8082 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 fallbacks=none topN=30 documents=34 13:09:30+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 model=mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 url=http://local-llm-server-02:8082/v1/rerank topN=30 documents=34 ``` ... ``` 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 response status=200 ok=true 13:09:36+00:00 debug [memory-external-reranker] candidate=local-llm-server-02-8082/mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0 success: 30 documents reranked ``` The reranker plugin loaded as a gateway plugin, received 34 candidate documents, sent them to the remote endpoint, and returned 30 reranked results. - Observed result after fix: better search results from memory - What was not tested: QMD, LanceDB, other memory systems - Proof limitations or environment constraints: - Before evidence (optional but encouraged):
Real behavior proof guidance config json fragments agents:defaults: ``` "memorySearch": { "enabled": true, "provider": "openai-compatible", "model": "Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "query": { "hybrid": { "enabled": true, "vectorWeight": 0.7, "textWeight": 0.3, "mmr": { "enabled": true, "lambda": 0.7, "provider": "memory-external-reranker", "fallback": "memory-mmr" } } }, "remote": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8081/v1", "apiKey": { "source": "env", "provider": "default", "id": "LOCAL_LLM_02_KEY" }, "batch": { "enabled": false }, "nonBatchConcurrency": 3 }, } ``` models:providers: ``` "local-llm-server-02-8082": { "baseUrl": "http://local-llm-server-02:8082", "models": [ { "id": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "name": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "input": [ "text" ] } ] } ``` plugins:entries: ``` "memory-external-reranker": { "enabled": true, "config": { "provider": "local-llm-server-02-8082", "model": "mradermacher/Qwen3-Reranker-4B-GGUF:Q8_0", "topN": 30 } }, ``` - Real environment tested: multiple test VMs. I've been running it on my production Openclaw instance for over a week. - Exact steps or command run after this patch: Enable and configure the memory-external-reranker plugin, index memory, perform a memory search like "What do you remember about the Lucid Air?" See attached screenshot.
## Tests and validation Which commands did you run? pnpm build && pnpm check && pnpm test pnpm test:extension memory-external-reranker pnpm test:extension memory-mmr What regression coverage was added or updated? Full tests added for both MMR plugin and new external reranker plugin. What failed before this fix, if known? If no test was added, why not?
Testing guidance To do full testing, an embedding model and reranking model are needed. I use llamacpp on localhost and update openclaw.json to point to the local models. ``` ./llama-server -hf Qwen/Qwen3-Embedding-0.6B-GGUF --port 8081 --embedding ./llama-server -hf ggml-org/Qwen3-Reranker-0.6B-Q8_0-GGUF --port 8082 --embedding --reranking --pooling rank ```
## Risk checklist Did user-visible behavior change? No Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? Yes. New config entries added Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? No. Uses existing patterns and code to call external reranker. What is the highest-risk area? Memory search results are not reranked by either the external reranker nor MMR. How is that risk mitigated? MMR is a bundled plugin. As long as mmr is a buldled plugin and loaded by default, the risk is minimal.
Risk guidance Minimal risk once MMR is a bundled plugin.
## Current review state What is the next action? What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof? Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?
Review state guidance Keep this as the durable state for review progress. If useful information appears in comments, fold the current next action or blocker back here so maintainers and ClawSweeper do not need to reconstruct state from comment history.

target.title: fix(memory-core): preserve sibling supplement results when one search rejects (#77897)
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #77897. `searchMemoryCorpusSupplements` (extensions/memory-core/src/tools.shared.ts) used `Promise.all` to fan out to every registered corpus supplement (`memory-wiki`, third-party plugin supplements, etc.). `Promise.all` is fail-fast: a single rejection discards every sibling's already-resolved results.

This patch swaps `Promise.all` → `Promise.allSettled`, then re-flattens the fulfilled values. Rejections are logged once per offender with the registering plugin id so operators can correlate to the responsible supplement.

## Behavior contract (mathematical guarantee)

Let `S` be the set of registered corpus supplements at call time. Let `S_ok ⊆ S` be those whose `search(params)` fulfills, and `S_err = S \ S_ok` those that reject. The function now satisfies:

```
result = sort_score_desc_then_path(⋃_{s ∈ S_ok} s.search(params)).slice(0, max(1, maxResults ?? 10))
```

In particular:
- `|S_err| ≥ 1` does not collapse `result` to `[]`. Sibling results survive.
- `|S_err| = |S|` returns `[]` (graceful degradation, not throw).
- `|S_err| = 0` is byte-identical to the prior behavior — same merge, same sort, same `maxResults` clamp.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** A single misbehaving memory corpus supplement plugin causes `memory_search corpus=all` to return `[]` instead of preserving sibling supplement hits. The user-visible symptom is silent retrieval loss with no trace of the offending plugin in logs.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at commit `0cc7b938ba6b7acfac935591bcd51ddddb46c5c1` on Node 22 / Darwin 25.2.0, exercising the production `memory_search` tool path through the live `openclaw/plugin-sdk/memory-core-host-runtime-core` SDK boundary. Two real corpus supplements (`healthy-wiki`, `broken-wiki`) registered through the public `registerMemoryCorpusSupplement(...)` SDK export — no mocks of the function under test, no mocks of the supplement registry, no stubs of `searchMemoryCorpusSupplements`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts -- --reporter=verbose -t "preserves sibling"` from the repository root, which compiles the production source via tsgo and drives `createMemorySearchToolOrThrow().execute(...)` against the registered live supplements.
- **Evidence after fix:** Captured terminal output from the `node` invocation above; the fenced console output below shows the integration test exercising the production tool path with one resolving and one rejecting supplement, and capturing both the surviving wiki hit and the warn line that names the offender:

  ```
   RUN  v4.1.5 /Users/shubh-trips/Documents/personal-project/open-source/openclaw

   ✓ |extension-memory| extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts > memory tools > preserves sibling supplement results when one corpus=all supplement rejects (#77897) 6ms

   Test Files  1 passed (1)
        Tests  1 passed | 15 skipped (16)
     Start at  03:25:56
     Duration  1.05s

  [test] passed 1 Vitest shard in 2.97s
  ```

  The runtime assertions captured by this `node` run (full source: `extensions/memory-core/src/tools.citations.test.ts:386`) are:

  - `details.results` from the production `createMemorySearchToolOrThrow().execute(...)` envelope contains `["wiki", "entities/healthy.md"]` from the resolving `healthy-wiki` supplement
  - `details.results` simultaneously contains `["memory", "MEMORY.md"]` from the local memory backend
  - `console.warn` was invoked exactly once with a runtime log line of the shape `memory-core: corpus supplement "broken-wiki" search failed; sibling results preserved (corpus backend unavailable).`
- **Observed result after fix:** The surviving healthy supplement hit `wiki/entities/healthy.md` is delivered to the agent alongside the local `memory/MEMORY.md` result, and the runtime log emits the named-offender warn line `corpus supplement "broken-wiki" search failed; sibling results preserved (corpus backend unavailable).` exactly once — no `[]` collapse and no rethrow. Pre-fix on the same setup with current `Promise.all`-based main, the resolving supplement's `entities/healthy.md` hit is discarded and the user-facing `details.results` returns empty alongside an unhandled rejection bubbling through the supplement fanout.
- **What was not tested:** Cross-process supplement registration (the public SDK supplement registry is currently a single-process in-memory map; no IPC supplement adapter exists today, so cross-process degradation behavior is out of scope for this bug).

## Changelog

Entry added under `## Unreleased` → `### Fixes`:

> Memory/search: keep results from healthy memory corpus supplements when one rejects, so a single misbehaving plugin no longer empties `memory_search corpus=all`; failures are warn-logged with the offending plugin id instead of poisoning sibling results. Fixes #77897. Thanks @lonexreb.

## Why this matters

Memory search is on the agent's hot read path. Before this fix, a single misbehaving wiki/corpus plugin could cause all `memory_search(corpus="all")` calls to look empty — the agent silently loses retrieval signal, and the bad plugin is hard to identify because results just disappear. After this fix, the failing plugin is named in the runtime warn log and sibling retrieval continues to work, so a noisy supplement degrades gracefully instead of poisoning the whole memory subsystem.

## Risk

- **Surface**: one function in one bundled extension. Get-by-lookup is unchanged (it iterates synchronously and returns the first hit, so a single throw was already isolated).
- **Compat**: `Promise.allSettled` is supported in Node 22+ (the repo's stated runtime).
- **Logging**: one `console.warn` per failing supplement per call; matches existing `memory-core:` log prefix used in `dreaming-narrative.ts`.

Closes #77897

target.title: fix: channels with only {enabled: true} config are skipped at startup
target.body:
## Problem

`hasMeaningfulChannelConfig` in `src/channels/config-presence.ts` filters out channel configs that only contain `{"enabled": true}`. This causes channels to be excluded from `listPotentialConfiguredChannelIds`, and thus not loaded by the Gateway at startup.

`qqbot` channel works because it also has `appId` and `clientSecret` keys. But any channel that only sets `enabled: true` is silently skipped.

## Fix

Added early return `if (value.enabled === true) return true` — if a channel is explicitly enabled, it should always be considered meaningfully configured.

## Before/After

| Config | Before | After |
|--------|--------|-------|
| `{"enabled": true}` | ❌ skipped | ✅ loaded |
| `{"enabled": true, "appId": "x"}` | ✅ loaded | ✅ loaded |
| `{"enabled": false}` | ❌ skipped | ❌ skipped |
| `{}` | ❌ skipped | ❌ skipped |

target.title: docs(skills/coding-agent): worktree-isolate long spawns + watchdog-trap mitigations
target.body:
## Summary

Adds operator-facing guidance to defuse a recurring failure mode in
multi-agent orchestration: a watchdog-reported "terminated" spawn
that actually kept running and silently raced the parent agent on a
shared working tree.

We hit this in real downstream work — the parent agent received a
harness \`produced no output for 180s and was terminated\` message,
treated the spawn slot as free, started doing per-file integration
work in the same project root, and then spent ~30 minutes chasing
phantom regressions before checking \`pgrep\` and discovering the
"killed" worker was still alive and overwriting staged changes.

This PR is the operator-side workaround. A complementary runtime
change to relabel the watchdog message itself (something like
"watchdog tripped — process may still be running; verify with \`ps -p
<PID>\`") would fix the root trap; the skill changes here are the
documented mitigations until that ships.

## Changes (all in \`skills/coding-agent/SKILL.md\`)

1. **Claude Code section** now distinguishes foreground \`--print\`
   (short tasks) from background long-running, where
   \`--output-format stream-json\` is preferred so the harness watchdog
   sees continuous liveness instead of silently ticking down on
   \`--print\`'s buffered output.

2. **New section: "Verifying a spawn is actually dead"** explains
   that the watchdog message is advisory, lists the working-tree
   symptoms of a rogue spawn racing the parent (files reverting,
   phantom untracked files, edits disappearing, unrelated test
   failures), and prescribes \`pgrep <agent-binary>\` as the first
   diagnostic step.

3. **Progress Updates section** gets one new bullet: don't treat a
   harness "terminated" message as a confirmed kill; verify before
   spawning a replacement. Points readers at the new section.

4. **Two new Rules entries** codify the above as hard rules:
   - **#12** — Worktree-isolate any long-running spawn that would
     otherwise share a working tree with the parent agent.
   - **#13** — Watchdog "terminated" messages are advisory, not
     authoritative. Verify with \`ps -p\` / \`pgrep\`. If \`--print\`-mode
     Claude Code spawns repeatedly trip the watchdog, switch to
     \`--output-format stream-json\`.

## Why these specifically

- \`--output-format stream-json\` for long backgrounded Claude Code is
  the prevention; \`pgrep + worktree isolation\` is the cure. Both belong
  in this skill because both are operator-facing CLI patterns.
- The new section sits between Progress Updates and the Rules block
  because it's a diagnostic playbook, not a rule.
- The Rules block already has 11 entries; #12 and #13 extend the
  existing \`~/Projects/openclaw/\` and \`~/.openclaw/\` "blast-radius"
  rules with the analogous "any project working tree the parent
  agent is using" rule.

## Test plan

- [ ] Read the diff (62 lines added, 1 deleted; one file).
- [ ] Confirm the new patterns don't conflict with existing skills
      anywhere else in the registry.
- [ ] If the runtime team wants to relabel the watchdog message at
      the source, this PR can land first as a workaround and that
      change can supersede the diagnostic section later.

target.title: fix(session): persist memory on auto daily/idle rollover
target.body:
## Summary

Auto-rollover (daily or idle stale-detection) takes a different code path than /reset and /new commands, so the bundled session-memory hook never sees a command:reset event and pre-reset transcripts go unwritten. Low-activity agents that get rolled over at 4am without ever compacting or typing a reset command silently lose their daily memory entry.

This PR fires the same internal command:reset hook on stale rollover with a commandSource of 'auto:daily' or 'auto:idle' so existing listeners (including the bundled session-memory handler) persist memory for automatic reset paths too. Guarded against firing on explicit /reset (handled by emitResetCommandHooks) and on fresh sessions.

target.title: fix: normalize visible assistant output before delivery
target.body:
## Summary
Gemma was sometimes leaking hidden internal text into Discord replies.
This PR makes OpenClaw clean the final reply in one shared place before sending it.
It adds regression tests for the real bad cases we saw, including leaked `<channel|>` markers and runaway repeated replies.
The fix is shared, not Gemma-only, so every delivery path uses the same cleanup.


## What changed
- teach the shared visible-text normalizer to:
  - strip leaked `<channel|>` control delimiters when they behave like leaked scaffolding
  - preserve literal `<channel|>` mentions in ordinary prose
  - preserve explicitly requested quoted targets, including full repeated strings and visible outputs that end with `<channel|>`
  - honor explicit repeat-count requests like `repeat "ABCD-1234" exactly twice`
  - drop truncated trailing leaked delimiters after long internal preambles
  - keep real leaks visible even when the preamble previously mentioned `<channel|>` literally
  - prefer the minimal repeated unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer repeats a mistakenly doubled exact target many times
  - preserve meaningful leading indentation in delivery mode while still trimming incidental single-space padding
- apply delivery-mode sanitization in the final delivery paths, while keeping stream-update sanitization lighter
- defer chunked `text_end` delivery until the final flush so partial raw scaffold chunks are never emitted early
- mark chunked `message_end` drains as final delivery so the delivery sanitizer runs on the last emitted chunks
- preserve plain streamed chunks without directive parsing when no inline directives are present
- preserve legacy `text_end` reply directives and keep intermediate chunk drains in history mode until final delivery
- reset `blockState` when `text_end` replaces the block buffer with already-sanitized final text, so stale reasoning-tag state cannot strip the final answer on flush

## Regression tests added first
- long prose that literally mentions `<channel|>` stays intact
- `plan: <channel|>visible` is treated as leaked scaffolding
- explicitly requested repeated outputs stay intact when named literally, in quotes, or via repeat-count instructions
- explicitly requested visible outputs that end with `<channel|>` stay intact
- literal marker explanations after an initial leaked delimiter stay intact
- truncated leaked delimiters after long preambles are dropped
- leaked delimiters are still stripped when earlier preamble text mentioned `<channel|>` literally
- repeated exact-target salvage prefers the minimal unit when a no-delimiter runaway answer duplicates the target in the model preamble and then repeats it many times
- legacy `text_end` reply directives still work
- chunked `message_end` drains are marked as final delivery
- intermediate chunk drains keep indentation before `text_end`
- delivery-mode sanitization keeps meaningful leading indentation while still trimming incidental padding
- chunk-boundary reasoning tags do not eat the final flushed answer

## Verification
- failing regressions were added first and reproduced locally before the fixes
- focused tests:
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts`
  - `pnpm exec vitest run src/shared/text/repeated-visible-suffix.test.ts src/shared/text/assistant-visible-text.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-utils.strip-model-special-tokens.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.reply-tags.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-pi-session.keeps-indented-fenced-blocks-intact.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/reply-utils.test.ts`
- `pnpm build`
- live Discord probe on `vllm/gemma4-e4b` after restarting the local gateway on this branch:
  - Looper prompt: `reply with exactly pr68986-live2-1776657698 and nothing else`
  - Bob visible Discord reply: `pr68986-live2-1776657698`

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: fix(ios): stabilize chat streaming layout and session flow
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: iOS chat streaming could attach to a synthetic placeholder lane, churn during initial history/bootstrap, and reparse streaming content in ways that caused flicker or session drift.
- Why it matters: active conversations can show unstable streaming UI, lose the correct lane/session association, or visibly flicker while tokens arrive.
- What changed: scoped streaming to the active session key, hardened stream acceptance/initial load behavior, and stabilized layout parsing/visibility updates.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol changes, no new model behavior. This fix lives in the shared `OpenClawChatUI` layer used by both iOS and macOS. The bug (cross-run UI drift during concurrent session-key agent events) affects any client using `ChatViewModel`; macOS uses the same chat view and benefits equally, but the fix is not platform-specific — it guards shared streaming state.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: targeted shared-package tests plus iOS build validation.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #40985
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: streaming state was not consistently tied to the active session key, and downstream UI/layout updates re-parsed or re-evaluated visibility in ways that amplified bootstrap churn into visible instability.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was insufficient coverage around session-scoped streaming updates plus layout stability under rapid token updates/history refresh.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): this branch supersedes #40985 by fixing the broader lane/acceptance/layout causes instead of only the earlier visible symptom.
- Why this regressed now: incremental fixes addressed isolated symptoms while the newer streaming lane introduced more state transitions that needed session-scoped and layout-stable handling.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: the issue was not isolated to one text-rendering widget; it involved session scoping, bootstrap timing, and layout parsing together.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/ChatViewModelTests.swift`
  - `apps/shared/OpenClawKit/Tests/OpenClawKitTests/AssistantTextParserTests.swift`
- Scenario the test should lock in: session-scoped streaming updates must survive bootstrap/history churn and preserve stable parsed output/visibility while tokens stream.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: the bug lives in shared chat state/parsing logic, so package-level tests cover the core behavior without requiring a full end-to-end harness.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): targeted parser/view-model tests were added/expanded for this branch.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- iOS chat streaming stays attached to the active session instead of a synthetic placeholder lane.
- Streaming text/layout behaves more stably during initial load/history refresh.
- Visible flicker/churn from repeated parse/visibility mismatches is reduced.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS local dev environment
- Runtime/container: shared OpenClawKit package + iOS app build
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): iOS chat UI / streaming flow
- Relevant config (redacted): local iOS simulator build

### Steps

1. Build the shared package and iOS app from this branch.
2. Open a chat session and trigger a streaming reply.
3. Observe initial history/bootstrap plus active token streaming.

### Expected

- Streaming binds to the active session.
- Bootstrap/history churn does not destabilize the active stream.
- Layout remains visually stable while tokens arrive.

### Actual

- Matched expected in targeted tests and local build validation.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests`
  - `swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests`
  - `xcodegen generate`
  - `xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClaw -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build`
- Edge cases checked:
  - stream acceptance during bootstrap/history load
  - parser stability under repeated streaming updates
- What you did **not** verify:
  - full repository CI matrix
  - non-iOS clients

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
  - All 19 review threads (P1/P2 from `chatgpt-codex-connector[bot]` + `copilot-pull-request-reviewer`) resolved in this update.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A



## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: chat-state timing bugs can reappear under bootstrapping or external-final sync edge cases.
  - Mitigation: this patch scopes state to the active session and adds targeted parser/view-model coverage around the churn paths.



## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: iOS/shared OpenClawChatUI streaming state remains scoped to the active session, load recovery remains idempotent after a failed initial bootstrap plus successful manual refresh, and streaming text/tool state does not get cleared by a later view `load()` call.
- **Real environment tested**: macOS local dev environment, Xcode 26.5 SDKs, Swift Testing 1902, OpenClawKit shared package and generated iOS Xcode project from this PR head `591a724df2531d373fc8603f2faa323fc93bc053`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

```bash
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter ChatViewModelTests
swift test --package-path apps/shared/OpenClawKit --filter AssistantTextParserTests
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml
xcodebuild -project apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj -scheme OpenClawChatUI -destination 'generic/platform=iOS Simulator' CODE_SIGNING_ALLOWED=NO build
```

- **Evidence after fix**:

```text
ChatViewModelTests: Test run with 83 tests in 1 suite passed after 5.550 seconds.
AssistantTextParserTests: Test run with 7 tests in 1 suite passed after 0.001 seconds.
xcodegen generate --spec apps/ios/project.yml: Created project at /Users/eulices/projects/openclaw-pr50483/apps/ios/OpenClaw.xcodeproj
xcodebuild OpenClawChatUI generic iOS Simulator: ** BUILD SUCCEEDED **
```

- **Observed result after fix**: The recovered-load regression is covered by `successful refresh after failed load makes later load idempotent`, which passed after confirming a failed first bootstrap can recover through manual refresh and a later `load()` does not issue another history request or clear the active stream. The shared `OpenClawChatUI` target also built successfully for iOS Simulator from the generated project.
- **What was not tested**: Full `OpenClaw` app scheme build was attempted but blocked by local Xcode environment missing watchOS 26.5 for the embedded Watch app; the directly changed shared chat UI target was built instead. Full repository CI matrix was left to GitHub.

target.title: Telegram: allow native channel commands in explicitly allowed chats
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: Telegram `channel_post` updates could carry regular text, but native slash commands such as `/status` were not authorized
correctly in channel relay setups.
- Why it matters: OpenClaw bot-to-bot Telegram channel workflows could not use native commands even when the chat itself was explicitly
allowed.
- What changed: Native Telegram commands now accept `channel_post` inputs and authorize them by explicit chat allow status for the
receiving account.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): This PR does not redefine sender-scoped `groups[chatId].allowFrom` semantics for `channel_post`,
because Telegram does not expose the originating human sender on that surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Integrations
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Related #49062

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Telegram native commands such as `/status` now work from explicitly allowed `channel_post` chats.
- Disallowed Telegram channel chats still receive `"This group is not allowed."`
- Regular group/DM sender-based command authorization behavior is unchanged.

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes`)
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`)
- New/changed network calls? (`No`)
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes`)
- Data access scope changed? (`No`)
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation:

This PR enables Telegram native command execution from explicitly allowed channel chats, which is a command execution surface change. The
risk is bounded by existing explicit chat allow checks. Disallowed chats remain blocked, and regression tests cover both allowed and denied
channel cases as well as `commands.allowFrom.telegram` precedence.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

OpenClaw version: 2026.3.13
Operating system: all OS
Install method: npm global
Model: gpt-5.4
Provider / routing chain: chatgpt oauth



### Steps

1. Configure a Telegram account with the target channel explicitly allowed.
2. Send /status as a channel_post in that Telegram channel.
3. Observe native command authorization and reply behavior.

### Expected

- Explicitly allowed channel chats can execute native commands such as /status.

### Actual

- Before this fix, the command was rejected or dropped on channel_post.
- After this fix, the command is authorized by explicit chat allow and executes normally.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios:
    - /status from an explicitly allowed Telegram channel now succeeds.
    - Disallowed channel chats remain blocked by chat allowlist checks.
- Edge cases checked:
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not block explicitly allowed channel_post commands.
    - commands.allowFrom.telegram does not bypass denied channel_post chat allowlists.
- What you did not verify:
    - Sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom semantics for channel_post
    - Live plugin command behavior on Telegram channels
    - Webhook-specific Telegram deployment paths

## Review Conversations

- [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for
maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (Yes)
- Config/env changes? (No)
- Migration needed? (No)
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Failure Recovery (if this breaks)

- How to disable/revert this change quickly:
    - Revert this PR, or disable Telegram native commands temporarily with channels.telegram.commands.native: false
- Files/config to restore:
    - extensions/telegram/src/bot-native-commands.ts
- Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for:
    - Explicitly allowed Telegram channel /status still returns "You are not authorized to use this command."
    - Disallowed channel chats start executing native commands unexpectedly

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write None.

- Risk:
    - channel_post authorization now uses explicit chat allow rather than sender-scoped identity.
    - Mitigation:
        - This is limited to Telegram channel_post, where Telegram does not expose the originating human sender.
        - Explicit chat allow is still required.
        - Regression tests cover both allow and deny behavior.
- Risk:
    - Future contributors may assume sender-scoped groups[chatId].allowFrom also applies to channel_post.
    - Mitigation:
        - Scope is explicitly documented in this PR.
        - A separate follow-up issue will track channel_post sender-scoped allowlist semantics.

target.title: feat(llm): forward run context to model as opt-in request headers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR adds optional request headers to model API calls so providers can forward a few runtime context fields — a run ID, the inbound channel, and the operation kind (real message / heartbeat / scheduled job) — as opt-in headers for cost attribution behind a proxy.

## Changes

- **packages/llm-core/src/types.ts** — add `runContextHeaders` opt-in config type
- **src/config/types.models.ts** — expose the new config field
- **src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/run/attempt.ts** — pass run context to model call
- **src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/stream-resolution.ts** — pass run context on stream path
- **src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts** — inject headers when opt-in is enabled

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

**Behavior or issue addressed**: Forward run context (run id, channel, operation kind) to the model endpoint as opt-in request headers so a proxy can attribute costs per run/channel.

**Real environment tested**: Linux x86_64, Node 24, OpenClaw local dev instance

**Exact steps or command run after this patch**: `node --max-old-space-size=4096 node_modules/.bin/vitest run packages/llm-core --reporter=verbose`

**Evidence after fix**: Console output confirms `run-context-headers` feature works correctly — all relevant tests pass and header injection paths are exercised:

```
✓ packages/llm-core > llm run context headers > injects headers when opt-in enabled  45ms
✓ packages/llm-core > llm run context headers > skips headers when not configured    32ms
✓ packages/llm-core (2 tests) 123ms
```

**Observed result after fix**: The `X-OpenClaw-Run-Id`, `X-OpenClaw-Channel`, and `X-OpenClaw-Operation` headers are present in upstream model requests when the `runContextHeaders` opt-in config is enabled; no extra headers are sent when the option is absent.

**What was not tested**: End-to-end verification with an actual HTTP proxy server; header injection verified via unit tests against the transport layer only.

target.title: Preserve shared QA suite flags across runners
target.body:
## Summary

- Stacked on #91502.
- Preserves shared QA suite selections across both built-in suite runners by forwarding `--channel-driver crabline --channel telegram` and `--enable-plugin` into the existing isolated runner wrapper.
- Keeps the channel driver meaning scoped to the openclaw/crabline messaging/channel SDK; this PR does not add Canonical Multipass VM orchestration behavior, guest command features, or a VM-backed channel driver.
- Leaves the real runner-specific exceptions unchanged: VM resource flags remain Multipass-only, and `--cli-auth-mode` / `--preflight` remain host-only.
- Review focus: whether these are the right parity boundaries for suite-level flags versus runner-lifecycle or credential-sensitive flags.

## Linked context

Stacked on openclaw/openclaw#91502.
Related openclaw/crabline#1.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: shared QA suite selections no longer get dropped or rejected when using `qa suite --runner multipass`; the existing isolated runner wrapper preserves them for the guest suite command.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on `codex/multipass-channel-driver-runner-parity`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  - `git diff --check`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check CHANGELOG.md extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/multipass.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/multipass.runtime.test.ts`
  - `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/multipass.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.summary-json.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/qa-transport-registry.test.ts`
- Evidence after fix: focused Vitest run passed 6 files / 117 tests; formatting, whitespace, and extension typecheck passed.
- Observed result after fix: `runQaSuiteCommand` now passes channel-driver selection and plugin enablements to `runQaMultipass`, and `createQaMultipassPlan` renders those selections into the generated guest `pnpm openclaw qa suite` command.
- What was not tested: a live run of the existing isolated runner wrapper and live Telegram SDK-channel e2e assertions.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: this is scoped to deterministic command construction and CLI routing; live isolated-runner validation can run after reviewer agreement on the parity boundary.
- Before evidence: #91502 rejected `--channel-driver crabline` with `--runner multipass`, and the isolated runner wrapper did not forward `--enable-plugin`.

## Tests and validation

- Added CLI runtime coverage for forwarding channel-driver selection and plugin enablements to `runQaMultipass`.
- Added isolated-runner command construction coverage for generated guest command flags.
- Validation run locally:
  - `git diff --check`
  - `pnpm exec oxfmt --check CHANGELOG.md extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/multipass.runtime.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/multipass.runtime.test.ts`
  - `pnpm tsgo:extensions`
  - `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/cli.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/multipass.runtime.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/crabline-channel-driver.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.summary-json.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/suite.test.ts extensions/qa-lab/src/qa-transport-registry.test.ts`

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? `Yes`: `qa suite --runner multipass` now accepts and preserves shared channel-driver and plugin enablement flags.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? `No`.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? `No`.

What is the highest-risk area? Misclassifying a runner-specific flag as shared suite semantics.

How is that risk mitigated? The PR only forwards deterministic suite selections; credential-sensitive auth mode and preflight artifact behavior stay host-only, and VM sizing flags stay Multipass-only.

## Current review state

Next action: review after #91502.

Still waiting on: #91502 and openclaw/crabline#1.

Bot/reviewer comments addressed: follow-up to maintainer question about runner flag parity; repo identity and channel-driver id updated from `multipass` to `crabline`.

target.title: fix(skills): warn for untrusted local sources
target.body:
Fixes #57535

## Problem summary
Local skills from workspace, `.agents`, managed, and extra skill roots were shown as ordinary ready/blocked skills. Operators had no first-class status signal distinguishing ClawHub/bundled skills from local skill files that should be reviewed before enablement or invocation.

## Root cause
The skills loader already tracks each skill source, and ClawHub installs already write `.clawhub/origin.json`, but `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus` collapsed that into `source`/`bundled` only. CLI and Control UI status surfaces therefore had no trust metadata to render.

## Architectural reasoning
This keeps runtime loading semantics unchanged and adds trust classification at the status/reporting layer. Built-in skills are trusted as `openclaw-bundled`; valid ClawHub origin metadata is trusted as `clawhub`; reviewed local roots configured in `skills.load.trustedDirs` are trusted as `trusted-dir`; known local sources without either signal are marked `untrustedLocalSource` with a warning. The change is additive to the status API and avoids coupling core loader behavior to owner-specific skill ids.

## Testing performed
- `pnpm test src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm test src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts` passed.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 CHANGELOG.md src/agents/skills-status.ts src/agents/skills-status.test.ts src/commands/doctor-skills.test.ts src/cli/skills-cli.format.ts src/cli/skills-cli.test.ts src/config/types.skills.ts src/config/zod-schema.ts src/config/schema.help.ts src/config/schema.labels.ts src/config/schema.help.quality.test.ts ui/src/ui/types.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills-shared.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.ts ui/src/ui/views/skills.test.ts docs/tools/skills.md docs/tools/skills-config.md` passed.
- `pnpm lint:core` passed.
- `pnpm lint:docs` passed.
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx` passed.
- `pnpm tsgo:core` passed via a temporary `P:\` mapping to avoid the repo path-with-spaces issue.
- `pnpm tsgo:core:test` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm tsgo:test:ui` passed via the same temporary mapping.
- `pnpm config:docs:check` initially detected expected config baseline drift; after `pnpm config:docs:gen`, `pnpm config:docs:check` passed.
- `git diff --check upstream/main...HEAD` passed after clean rebase.
- `pnpm check:changed` passed locally. Blacksmith Testbox was unavailable because `blacksmith` is not installed on PATH.

`pnpm test:changed` was also run locally and selected a broad `unit-fast` shard; it failed in 19 unrelated files with existing Windows/path/host assumptions such as `/tmp` becoming `C:\tmp`, POSIX table glyph expectations on Windows output, A2UI scaffold 404 in `extensions/canvas`, and temp SQLite cleanup `EBUSY`. The focused touched-surface tests above passed.

`pnpm format:docs:check` could not be used as proof in this environment because the wrapper crashed on Node v22.15.0 with `TypeError: Cannot read properties of undefined (reading 'trim')` in `scripts/format-docs.mjs`; targeted `oxfmt --check`, `lint:docs`, and `docs:check-mdx` passed.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior or issue addressed: A temporary workspace skill without ClawHub origin metadata is now classified as an untrusted local source.
- Real environment tested: Windows checkout on branch `hemant/57535-untrusted-skill-sources`, Node v22.15.0, pnpm 10.33.2.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran a `pnpm exec tsx` script from the repo root that created a temporary `<workspace>/skills/local-review/SKILL.md`, called `buildWorkspaceSkillStatus(workspace)`, and rendered `formatSkillsCheck(report, {})`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): Copied terminal output from the local run:

```json
{
  "name": "local-review",
  "source": "openclaw-workspace",
  "trustSource": "local",
  "untrustedLocalSource": true,
  "hasWarning": true
}
```

```text
Untrusted local sources
local-review
Add the directory to skills.load.trustedDirs only if you trust this workspace-owned skill source.
```

- Observed result after fix: Local skill loading still succeeded and the status/check surface warned before users enable or invoke the local skill.
- What was not tested: No browser screenshot was captured because this is a status/API/CLI/UI-rendering change covered by jsdom UI tests rather than a visual layout change.

## Risk analysis
Low-to-medium. The classification is additive and synchronous, using small local origin/trusted-dir checks during status generation. It does not alter skill discovery, precedence, eligibility, model visibility, command visibility, or execution. Main residual risk is users seeing new warnings for legitimate local/plugin-provided skill roots until they add reviewed directories to `skills.load.trustedDirs`.

## Backward compatibility
Backward-compatible. Existing skill config continues to load the same skills. New `SkillStatusEntry` fields are additive, and `skills.load.trustedDirs` is optional. Existing `skills.load.allowSymlinkTargets` behavior is unchanged; docs now clarify that it controls loader containment while `trustedDirs` controls warning suppression.

## Screenshots/logs
No screenshots. Relevant command output is summarized above.

target.title: feat(plugins): expose ACP spawn and prompt in plugin runtime
target.body:
## Summary

Plugins can orchestrate subagents via `api.runtime.subagent.*` but have no way to dispatch ACP-backed agent sessions with **channel delivery**. This PR adds `api.runtime.acp.spawn()` and `api.runtime.acp.prompt()` to the plugin runtime, gated behind `plugins.entries.<id>.acp.allowSpawn` (per-plugin opt-in, default `false`).

## Why this is needed (reopening #65022)

Issue #65022 was closed with the assessment that `plugin-sdk/acp-runtime` already exposes `getAcpSessionManager`, so plugins can call `initializeSession()` + `runTurn()`. However, **`runTurn()` does not deliver output to channels**:

| API | Executes ACP | Delivers to Discord/Telegram/etc. |
|---|---|---|
| `AcpSessionManager.runTurn()` | ✅ | ❌ |
| `callGateway({ method: "agent", deliver: true })` | ✅ | ✅ |
| `api.runtime.subagent.run({ deliver: true })` | ✅ | ✅ (but no `channel`/`threadId`/`to` params) |
| **`api.runtime.acp.prompt()`** (this PR) | ✅ | ✅ |

`runTurn()` is the low-level execution primitive — it runs the AI backend and emits events internally. Channel delivery requires the gateway agent pipeline (`src/agents/agent-command.ts`) which calls `runTurn()` → accumulates visible text → calls `deliverAgentCommandResult()`.

Native ACP spawn (`src/agents/acp-spawn.ts`) uses `callGateway({ method: "agent", deliver: true })` for exactly this reason. `SubagentRunParams` has `deliver?: boolean` but lacks `channel`, `threadId`, `to`, and `accountId` — so `subagent.run()` cannot target a specific thread either.

Without this PR, plugins must either fork OpenClaw or shell out to the CLI to get ACP output delivered to a channel.

## Prior art

- **#63176** — original PR (same patchset, open, stalled on review)
- **#65022** — tracking issue (closed prematurely; closing comment checked API availability but missed the delivery gap)

## What changed

- `api.runtime.acp.spawn(params, ctx)` — starts a new ACP session with thread binding
- `api.runtime.acp.prompt({ sessionKey, text, channel, accountId, threadId })` — sends a follow-up prompt into an existing ACP session via `callGateway({ method: "agent", deliver: true })`
- Both gated behind `plugins.entries.<id>.acp.allowSpawn: true` (default `false`)
- Types, mocks, and tests updated

## Files (7 files, +253 lines)

- `src/plugins/runtime/runtime-acp.runtime.ts` — lazy-loaded ACP runtime helper (new)
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.ts` — wire `acp` namespace into plugin runtime
- `src/plugins/runtime/types-core.ts` — `acp.spawn` and `acp.prompt` types
- `src/plugins/runtime/index.test.ts` — gate + delegation tests
- `src/config/types.plugins.ts` — per-plugin `plugins.entries.<id>.acp.allowSpawn` type
- `src/config/zod-schema.ts` — validation
- `src/plugin-sdk/test-helpers/plugin-runtime-mock.ts` — mock alignment

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: A trusted plugin can continue an ACP-backed task session through the Gateway agent delivery path so the agent response is delivered into the bound Discord thread, while untrusted plugins cannot call the ACP runtime helpers.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw fork build with the `task-dispatch` plugin enabled and per-plugin config `plugins.entries.task-dispatch.acp.allowSpawn: true`; Discord thread delivery through the Gateway agent pipeline.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**: Updated the local `openclaw.json` from the old global `plugins.allowAcpSpawn` flag to `plugins.entries.task-dispatch.acp.allowSpawn: true`, then ran `pnpm openclaw config validate`, `cd ~/.openclaw/extensions/task-dispatch && bun run typecheck`, and `bun run build:plugin`. The existing task-dispatch live ACP smoke below exercises the same `api.runtime.acp.prompt({ channel: "discord", threadId })` delivery path this PR exposes.
- **Evidence after fix**: Config/build output and live runtime logs:

```text
Config valid: ~/.openclaw/openclaw.json
$ tsc --noEmit
$ bun build src/plugin/index.ts --target=node --format=esm --outfile=build/plugin.mjs --external better-sqlite3
Bundled 4926 modules in 550ms
plugin.mjs  15.81 MB  (entry point)
```

```text
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=2f025c40 initializing ACP session harness=zeus-opencode
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=2f025c40 ACP session initialized in 1766ms
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=2f025c40 binding Discord thread channel=1479519205136138476
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=2f025c40 Discord binding resolved in 1189ms, threadId=1502803134609752165
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=2f025c40 starting ACP turn pollTimeout=120000ms
[POLL] got valid output after 2 polls (18 chars)
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=2f025c40 ACP turn completed in 9209ms, text=18 chars
Result: Discord thread contains agent reply. Task status: done.
Stored output: TD_STRICT_SMOKE_OK
```

Gateway delivery confirmation:

```text
discord: delivered 1 reply to channel:1502803134609752165
message processed: reason=acp_dispatch
```

- **Observed result after fix**: `task-dispatch` still typechecks and builds against the updated per-plugin ACP trust config, OpenClaw accepts the updated config, and the live ACP prompt path delivers the ACP reply into the Discord thread instead of leaving the thread empty.
- **What was not tested**: I did not run a fresh long-lived coding task after changing the trust key from global to per-plugin; the smoke evidence above is the existing task-dispatch live ACP delivery proof for the same runtime prompt path, plus fresh config/build validation for the per-plugin trust change.

### Before (`runTurn()` only — no delivery)

```text
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=89d24685 initializing ACP session harness=zeus-opencode
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=89d24685 ACP session initialized in 2301ms
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=89d24685 binding Discord thread channel=1479519205136138476
[DISPATCH.ACP] task=89d24685 Discord binding resolved in 691ms, threadId=1502783588985344012
[ACP.TURN] starting prompt session=agent:zeus-opencode:acp:e1f7a24f thread=1502783588985344012
[ACP.TURN] ACP completion resolved for run=9c03df2b
[POLL] timed out after 23 polls, returning lastText (0 chars)
Result: Discord thread empty. Task errored.
```

## Security

- `plugins.entries.<id>.acp.allowSpawn` gate (default `false`, checked on every call with the caller plugin id)
- Delegates to the same `callGateway({ method: "agent" })` path native ACP spawn uses
- ACP prompt turns are marked with `acpTurnSource: "manual_spawn"` so explicit plugin-initiated follow-ups use the same ACP turn semantics as native spawn
